coredata.dc:title: A fluorescence-enhanced method specific for furfural determination in Chinese Baijiu based on luminescent carbon dots and direct surface reaction
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Bai, Hao-Jie
Li, Hong-Wei
Li, Yi
Huang, Zhijiu
Liu, Sha
Duan, Xin-He
Wu, Yuqing
coredata.dc:description:
Detecting the furfural concentration in Baijiu can be used to assess the quality of Baijiu, allowing for the optimization of processing techniques and the enhancement of overall quality. In this paper, a fluorescence-enhanced method based on carbon dots (o-CDs) is developed for the furfural determination in Chinese Baijiu. In an environment full-filled with
· SO
4 ‐
and
· OH
, furfural undergone a direct surface reaction with the ortho-diamino groups at o-CDs. The created furan-based imidazole increased the surface electron density, leading an emission enhancement and color changes from orange to green. Thereby, a linear fluorescence response of o-CDs-TA to furfural is established in water with a detection limit of 30.5 nM. Finally, after ethanol correction it is used to determine furfural in Chinese Baijiu with high precision and reproducibility, providing a new strategy with low-cost and high sensitivity. In particular, the idea of covalently connecting target molecule to the CDs surface via the assistance of free radical opens a new avenue to merge the nanoscale and molecular realms through implementing chemical role into carbon nanostructures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Furfural
Furan-based imidazole
Baijiu
Determination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorometric determination of furfural in buffer
Emission enhancement of CDs via surface oxidation
Fluorescence response of o-CDs-TA to furfural in aqueous solution
Intrinsic responsive mechanism of o-CDs-TA to furfural
The determination of furfural in Chinese Baijiu

The financial supports from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21875085) and Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Development Plan Project (No. 20230204040YY) were appreciated.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of o-CDs; (b) fluorescence lifetimes of o-CDs; (c) FT-IR absorption spectra of o-CDs; (d) TEM image and (e) size distribution of o-CDs; (f) XPS survey spectra of o-CDs; Enlarged XPS of C1s (g), O1s (h), and N1s (i) of o-CDs.

Fluorescence spectra of (a) o-CDs-A (20 μL/mL o-CDs with 3 mM APS) and (b) o-CDs-TA (20 μL/mL o-CDs with 3 mM APS and 16 mM TMEDA); (c) the corresponding fluorescence intensity changes at 570 nm; UV–vis absorption spectra of (d) o-CDs-A and (e) o-CDs-TA; (f) the corresponding absorbance value changes at 410 nm and 600 nm (TMEDA and APS are respectively abbreviated T and A), in H3BO3–NaOH buffer (pH = 8.5; λex = 410 nm).

(a) The fluorescence spectra of o-CDs-TA-F, the mixture of o-CDs and furfural in the presence of TMEDA and APS, together with the control subjects in the absence of each option; (b) the corresponding picture of o-CDs-TA-F and the relevant control subjects under sunlight (up) and UV light (365 nm; bottom); (c) the fluorescence spectra of o-CDs-TA-F along with the concentration changes of furfural between 0 and 200 μM; (d) the corresponding picture of o-CDs-TA-F (0–200 μM) under sunlight (up) and UV light (bottom); (e) the corresponding fluorescence intensity changes at 520 nm (error bar in red); (f) the corresponding CIE chromaticity coordinates changes of o-CDs-TA-F (0–200 μM).

(a) Fluorescence spectra and (c) the corresponding intensity changes (520 nm) of o-CDs-TA in the presence of 100 μM furfural (FUL), furfural analogs, aldehyde (ALD), alcohol (ALC), esters (EST); (b) fluorescence spectra and (d) the corresponding intensity changes (520 nm) of o-CDs-TA-F(a) in the presence of 100 μM different kinds of furfural analogs, aldehyde (ALD), alcohol (ALC), esters (EST). (Furfural is abbreviated as FUL, 5-hydroxymethylfurfural as HMF, 5-nitrofurfural as NF, 5-methy furfural as MF, 3-furaldehyde as FLD, furan as FUR, formaldehyde as f-ALD, acetaldehyde as a-ALD, butyraldehyde as bu-ALD, isobutyraldehyde as i-b-ALD, valeraldehyde as v-ALD, benzaldehyde as be-ALD, methanol as m-ALC, isopropyl alcohol as i-pr-ALC, isobutyl alcohol as i-b-ALC, isopentyl alcohol as i-pe-ALC, methyl acetate as m-a-EST, ethyl acetate as e-a-EST, ethyl lactate as e-l-EST).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of o-CDs, m-CDs, and p-CDs (solid lines), and those of them in the presence of TMEDA and APS (dashed lines), and further in the presence of furfural (dotted lines), respectively; (b) The corresponding photographs of them under sunlight (up) and UV light (bottom) irradiation. (c) FT-IR absorption spectra of o-CDs, o-CDs-TA, o-CDs-TA-F and FUL. (d) EPR spectra of DMPO-
· SO
4 ‐
and DMPO-
· OH
adducts in the solution of TA, o-CDs-TA, being analyzed using CDMPO = 100 mM.

(a) Fluorescence spectra and (b) the corresponding intensity changes at 520 nm for o-CDs-TA, and o-CDs-TA with 100 μM furfural, in different volume fraction of ethanol-buffer, respectively. The fluorescence intensity changes of o-CDs-TA at 520 nm (I–I
0 ) upon adding different amounts of furfural (0–200 μM), the linear regression between I–I
0 and furfural concentrations, and the evaluations of furfural in different Baijiu samples after diluting to the corresponding ethanol fraction, which are performed in (c) 5 vol %, and (d) 10 vol % fractions of ethanol, respectively.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis route of o-CDs, the induced fluorescence changes, and the suggested surface reaction of o-CDs with furfural.
Schematic illustration of the possible reaction of furfural with o-CDs and impossible reaction with m-CDs and p-CDs, respectively.
Standard addition experiment for determination of furfural in Light Baijiu (n = 3).
Determination and recovery rates of furfural in different Baijiu using o-CDs-TA (n = 5).

head.ce:title: A fluorescence-enhanced method specific for furfural determination in Chinese Baijiu based on luminescent carbon dots and direct surface reaction

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Detecting the furfural concentration in Baijiu can be used to assess the quality of Baijiu, allowing for the optimization of processing techniques and the enhancement of overall quality. In this paper, a fluorescence-enhanced method based on carbon dots (o-CDs) is developed for the furfural determination in Chinese Baijiu. In an environment full-filled with
· SO
4 ‐
and
· OH
, furfural undergone a direct surface reaction with the ortho-diamino groups at o-CDs. The created furan-based imidazole increased the surface electron density, leading an emission enhancement and color changes from orange to green. Thereby, a linear fluorescence response of o-CDs-TA to furfural is established in water with a detection limit of 30.5 nM. Finally, after ethanol correction it is used to determine furfural in Chinese Baijiu with high precision and reproducibility, providing a new strategy with low-cost and high sensitivity. In particular, the idea of covalently connecting target molecule to the CDs surface via the assistance of free radical opens a new avenue to merge the nanoscale and molecular realms through implementing chemical role into carbon nanostructures.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Furfural, a by-product of sugar derived from the food processing methods of fermentation or heating [1], is predominantly formed via the Maillard reaction [2]. During the brewing process of Baijiu, pentose sugars in grain ingredients also produce furfural during fermentation and distillation. Furfural is an important flavor component in Baijiu, contributing to its unique taste and aroma. Detecting the furfural concentration in Baijiu can be used to assess the quality of Baijiu, allowing for the optimization of processing techniques and the enhancement of overall quality. Additionally, prolonged or excessive intake of furfural can be toxic to the central nervous system and liver in humans. Animal experiments also indicate that furfural has potential carcinogenic and reproductive toxic effects [3,4]. Therefore, the detection of furfural is also related to food safety. Due to the potential carcinogenicity and reproductive toxicity of furfural, experimental data on human are not available. It has been reported that furfural has an acute toxicity LD50 (rat oral) of 65 mg/kg and an LC50 (rat inhalation, 4 h) of 153 ppm [5]. Developing robust methods for the determination of furfural in alcoholic beverages is therefore both important and necessary.

Nowadays, the commonly used methods for furfural determination in beverages is liquid chromatography (LC) [6,7] high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8], LC-MS [9,10], electrochemical [11], and some-times, SERS analytical methods [12]. These methods are highly accuracy and reproducible, yet with drawbacks of complicated protocol for sample preparation and expensive instrumentation. Therefore, the easily performed methods, such as colorimetry and fluorescent sensors based on either chemicals or nanomaterials as quantum dots (QDs) or carbon dots (CDs), have been developed for low-cost and fast determinations of furfural in versatile beverages [13–15]. For example, Rico-Yuste et al. [13] constructed a polymeric film with disposable color-changes for furfural quantification in beer. In addition, using 4-hydroxylaminopropyl-7-methoxylcoumarin (HAMC) as a fluorescence probe Zhang et al. [14] developed a nitrone formation method for the furfural determination in foods. Using QDs of CaF2 and CdTe as fluorescence sensor Luo et al. reported a molecular-imprint polymer to directly recognize 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) via the host-guest interaction between β-CD and HMF [15]. In addition, taking furfural and organic acids as the key targets, a sensor array based on CDs was constructed by Li et al., which was applied to 41 different Chinese Baijiu [16].

CDs, as a kind of carbon nanomaterials with diameters in the range of 1–10 nm. They are primarily composed of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen, and exhibit excellent photoluminescent properties [17,18]. CDs have diverse preparation methods, such as electrochemical oxidation, laser ablation, and hydrothermal/solvent methods. The color and intensity of their luminescence can be monitored by adjusting the size, structure and doping elements [19,20]. Due to the unique structure and properties, they have been widely used in bioimaging [21], biosensors [22,23], chemical detection [24,25], and so on. In particular, CDs have shining in food determination. For example, Liao et al. used β-cyclodextrin-modified carbon nanoparticles for the detection of melamine in milk samples [26]. Dong et al. used two-photon carbon quantum dots (TP-CQDs) for the detection of tunicamycin in food [24]. In this paper, a fluorescent carbon dot with a large number of amino groups on the surface was chosen to determine furfural in foodstuffs. The method has good specificity due to the unique furfuryl imidazole generation. Besides, the newly developed method has the advantages of low-cost, high sensitivity and strong immunity to potential interferences in comparison to other researches for furfural determination (Table S1) [27–33]. Specifically, the target furfural was attached to the ortho-diamino group at the surface of CDs to produce a furan-based imidazole structure, which in turn led to the orange-emission changes to green and a fluorescent method specific for furfural determination in Chinese Baijiu (Scheme 1 ).

O-phenylenediamine, m-phenylenediamine, p-phenylenediamine, tetra-methylethylenediamine (TMEDA), furfural, 5-hydroxymethylfurfural, 5-nitrofurfural, 5-methyl furfural, 3-furaldehyde, furan, formaldehyde solution, acetaldehyde, butyraldehyde, iso-butyraldehyde, valeraldehyde, benzaldehyde, methanol, isopropyl alcohol, two methyl-l-propanol, methyl and ethyl acetate, ethyl lactate, and ethanol was purchased from Aladdin Reagent with the purity higher than 99 %, respectively. Citric acid (AR), and boric acid (AR) was bought from Beijing Chemical Factory, while ammonium persulfate (APS, AR) was purchased from Macklin Reagent. Baijiu samples were obtained from Luzhou Laojiao Group Co., LTD (China) with the furfural contents measured by HPLC.

An equipment of RF-5301PC was used to measure the fluorescence spectrum, while that of an UV-3600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was used for UV–vis absorption spectrum. Each spectrum was measured at 25 °C in a quartz cuvette (0.3 × 1.0 cm) three times in parallel. The images of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were measured using JEM-2200FS (Jeol Ltd. Japan), at a voltage of 200 kV. For that, the sample was prepared respectively via dropping on a carbon-coated copper grid and drying at room temperature. The spectrometer of FLS920 (Edinburgh Instruments, United Kingdom) was employed to test the decay curve of time-resolved emission (λex = 400 nm). Besides, an equipment (Thermo Fischer ESCALAB 250Xi) was adopted to measure the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Zeta-potential measurement was performed by Zetasizer Pro (Malvern Instruments, UK). An IR spectrometer (VERTEX 80V IR, Bruker) was employed to measure the corresponding Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectrum. For that, the sample was dropped on a tablet of potassium bromide, being then baked under a tungsten lamp for drying. The data of electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) were taken from a spectrometer (BRUKER E500 EPR) at room temperature, utilizing 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) to capture the active species. Besides, the HPLC of furfural in Baijiu sample was measured on high performance liquid chromatograph of LC-2030C 3D Puls (Jeol Ltd. Japan).

The synthesis of o-CDs, p-CDs, and m-CDs was performed based on the reported method [33]. Taken o-CDs as an example, in brief, a mixture of 5 mL of 2 mg/mL of o-phenylenediamine and 5 mL of 1 mM of citric acid, which was heated at 180 °C for 10 h in an autoclave coated with Teflon inside (25 mL). After cooling at ambient condition, the solution was filtered using a PES membrane (with 0.22 μm pore size, from Jin Teng Ltd., Tian Jin, China) to remove the precipitation, and then dialyzed in ultrapure water for purification (10 h, in a dialysis bag of 1000 Da). Finally, the products were lyophilized and reserved. The synthesis and purification procedures for p-CDs and m-CDs are similar as o-CDs.
The characterizations on o-CDs were performed on the instruments mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorometric determination of furfural in buffer

ce:section.ce:para:
Similar to the above experiments, a reaction system was constructed except a portion of the buffer was replaced with certain amount of ethanol, to ensure that the volume fraction of ethanol was 5 vol % or 10 vol % in the detection system. After 1h reaction at RT, the fluorescence spectrum (λex = 410 nm) of each solution was collected. Finally, plots of I
520
versus furfural concentration in 5 vol % or 10 vol % ethanol-buffer solutions were plotted, respectively, and the linear equation was evaluated accordingly.

To determine the furfural concentration in Baijiu, they were diluted to 5 vol % or 10 vol % ethanol fractions using H3BO3–NaOH buffer (10 mM, pH = 8.5) in a reaction system. After 1h incubation at RT, the corresponding I
520 was recorded. The furfural concentration in original Baijiu is calculated based on the working plots of 5 vol % or 10 vol %, after multiply the dilution factor.

The carbon dots of o-CDs were synthesized using o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and citric acid as carbon sources [34]. Upon excitation at 410 nm, a strong emission at 570 nm was observed (Fig. 1 a–red). Under varying pH conditions, the emission peak of o-CDs remained consistently at 570 nm, while the intensity changed obviously with the pH and is strong at basic conditions (Figure S1). Additionally, the lifetime of o-CDs was calculated to be 1.910 ns based on the decay curve of the 570 nm emission (Fig. 1b), which is in agreement with previous reports [35,36] and confirms the successful synthesis of luminescent o-CDs. The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1a, blue) displayed three peaks at 235, 290, and 420 nm, attributed to the π−π* transition of C=C, the n−π* transition of C=O, and the n−π* transition of C=N, respectively [37]. The presence of these peaks indicates that there are numerous unsaturated bonds and π-electrons within the carbon dots.

In addition, FT-IR was applied to identify the functional groups present in the synthesized o-CDs (Fig. 1c). The peaks at 3382 cm−1 and 3165 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching modes of O–H or N–H groups, respectively. The peaks at 1401 cm−1 corresponds to the bending mode of N–H groups on the surface of o-CDs. Furthermore, the peak at 1095 cm−1 is associated with the stretching mode of C–N bond. The presence of these groups suggests that, following hydrothermal synthesis, a significant number of surplus amino groups remain on the surface of o-CDs. The peak at 1629 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching mode of C=O bonds, which is likely derived from citric acid in the synthesis ingredients. Additionally, the peak at 1533 cm−1 is associated with C–C and C=N bonds, and the peak at 749 cm−1 is generated by the ortho-diamino groups situated on the surface of o-CDs [38,39]. These results confirm that the obtained CDs are coated with amine and carboxyl groups at surface. The morphology and size of the o-CDs were further characterized using TEM images, which revealed that the o-CDs were small, well-dispersed spheres (Fig. 1d). A size distribution ranging from 1.04 to 3.62 nm, with an average particle size of 2.08 nm, was determined based on the analysis of 200 particles (n = 200; Fig. 1e).

The XPS analysis of o-CDs, as presented in Fig. 1f, revealed three distinct peaks corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively, confirming the presence of the essential elements. The atomic percentages were determined to be 50.49 %, 14.65 %, and 34.86 % for carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, respectively. The chemical states of these elements were further analyzed. In the detailed XPS of C1s (Fig. 1g), o-CDs exhibited three peaks at 284.6, 285.3 and 288.3 eV, representing different chemical states of carbon and their respective proportions: C–C/C=C (33.9 %), C–N/C=N (52.9 %) and C=O (13.2 %). The N1s XPS (Fig. 1h) clearly resolves two peaks, representing different nitrogen states: pyridine N (C–N=C, 24.8 %) at 398.6 eV and amino N (C–NH2, 75.2 %) at 399.6 eV. Additionally, in the O1s XPS (Fig. 1i), two peaks were observed at 531.4 eV for C=O and 532.6 eV for C–O–H/C–O–C, with respective contents of 23.2 % and 76.8 % [40,41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emission enhancement of CDs via surface oxidation
ce:section.ce:para: Bao et al. have found that surface oxidation introduces surface defects to CDs, which are crucial for their photoluminescence. These defects create diverse emission sites, enhancing their emission properties [42]. During hydrothermal synthesis, various oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups readily form at the edges and/or surfaces of CDs. These groups serves as sites for peripheral n-π* transition, preventing the recombination of surface-confined electrons and holes. This process contributes to a higher fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CDs [43].

Ammonium persulfate (APS) is a well-known oxidation agent that generates free radical, and the presence of tetra-methylethylenediamine (TMEDA) can catalytically accelerate the homolytic cleavage of APS to produce free radicals [44]. Consequently, in this research, APS and TMEDA will be introduced into the system to induce surface oxidation and enhance the emission enhancement of o-CDs. As observed, when APS is added to a solution containing o-CDs, the emission increases significantly within a short time (<0.5 h); however, it beings to quench gradually over time (Fig. 2 a–c). In contrast, when both APS and TMEDA are present, the emission of o-CDs increases substantially within 0.5 h; but it remains unchanged with time (Fig. 2b and c).

UV–vis absorption spectra were also measured in the presence of either APS (Fig. 2d) or both APS and TMEDA (Fig. 2e), respectively. Notably, the absorption band at 410 nm of o-CDs (Fig. 2f) consistently changes with the behavior of fluorescence emissions (Fig. 2c), while the absorption band at 600 nm changes differently: in the presence of separate APS, it increases gradually over the entire testing period; while the presence of both of APS and TMEDA keeps the emission almost unchanged.

Given that surface oxidation and core size are key factors in determining the luminescence properties of CDs, the enhancement in emission can be attributed to the surface oxidation upon the addition of APS. OPD can be oxidized by H2O2 or heavy metal ions to produce 2,3–diaminophenazine, which has yellow emission [45]. Therefore, when only APS is added, its strong oxidizing properties led to the polymerization of CDs, indicating the formation of an o-diamine structure, resulting in the emission quenching [46,47]. Additionally, the appearance of a new characteristic absorption peak around 600 nm suggests the creation of new substance or functional groups during this process.

In contrast, when TMEDA is further introduced, the fluorescence of o-CDs becomes less sensitive, which may be due to TMEDA prevents APS from polymerizing amine on the surface of CDs. Consequently, in this case, the absorption of o-CDs-TA at 600 nm remains unchanged, denying the production of new substances or functional groups. The results indicate that the addition of further TMEDA moderates the reaction system, avoids excessive oxidation by APS at the surface of o-CDs.

To further investigate the origination of these emission changes, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectrum was measured for the free radical generated in the systems upon APS and TMEDA additions (Figure S2). When only APS is present, no signal of free radical is generated, suggesting APS works only as an oxidant. However, in the presence of TMEDA, a large number of free radicals are apparently generated for APS. Such difference suggests it is TMEDA assist APS to generate free radicals, which avoid excessive oxidation occur at the surface of o-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of o-CDs-TA to furfural in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: An interesting reaction occurs between o-CDs and furfural in the presence of both TMEDA and APS (Fig. 3 a). Under excitation of 410 nm, the addition of furfural causes a blue-shift in original fluorescence emission of o-CDs from 570 nm to 520 nm, accompanied by an increase in fluorescence intensity. Such reaction system is defined as o-CDs-TA-F. Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig. 3a and b, the green emission at 520 nm is not observed when either of the reactants above is absent, indicating the necessity of each in the furfural detection.

To ascertain the selectivity of the novel method for furfural detection in distinguishing furfural analogs, selective experiments were conducted. Upon the addition of 100 μM of each compound to a solution containing o-CDs-TA, substituting for furfural (Fig. 4 a–c), it was observed that only 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) elicited a fluorescence enhancement and blue-shift akin to those induced by furfural. In contrast, 5-nitrofurfural (NF) resulted in a faint blue-shift with no fluorescence enhancement. Other analogs of furfural, such as 5-methyl furfural, 3-furaldehyde, and furan, did not alter the fluorescence of o-CDs-TA. These findings indicate that the newly developed assay for furfural exhibits the capability to differentiate between furfural and its structural congeners.

To assess the impact of the complex constituents, present in Chinese Baijiu on the detection of furfural using o-CDs-TA, a variety of aldehydes (ALD), alcohols (ALC), and esters (EST) commonly found in Chinese Baijiu were chosen for the analysis. The data revealed that these compounds exerted minimal influence on the fluorescence properties of o-CDs-TA.

Furthermore, to broaden the practical application of the developed method as a fluorescence sensor for the determination of furfural, anti-interference experiments were carried out by mixing 100 μM furfural with an equivalent concentration of potential interference (Fig. 4b–d). As depicted, the influence of these substances on the detection of furfural was insignificant, with the exception of 5-hydroxymethylfurfural and 5-nitrofurfural. However, since both of them are not commonly encountered in Chinese Baijiu [49], their impact on the determination of furfural in practical scenarios can be disregarded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intrinsic responsive mechanism of o-CDs-TA to furfural
ce:section.ce:para: To reveal the key factors influencing the reaction of furfural with o-CDs, a set of control experiments was conducted with the assistance of TMEDA and APS. In these experiments, m-phenylenediamine or p-phenylenediamine was used as a substitute for o-phenylenediamine as the carbon resource to fabricate CDs. The resulting m-CDs and p-CDs were then utilized for the determination of furfural, following the same procedure as with o-CDs (Fig. 5 a). As illustrated, upon the addition of TMEDA and APS to solutions containing m-CDs or p-CDs, the emission intensity of these CDs either increased or decreased, even larger than that of o-CDs (Fig. 5a, dashed lines). This indicated a more pronounced reaction for m-CDs and p-CDs compared to o-CDs.

Furthermore, when 100 μM furfural was added to the above solutions respectively, the fluorescence signal of m-CDs-TA and p-CDs-TA showed minimal change, whereas the fluorescence of o-CDs-TA significantly enhanced and exhibited a large blue-shifted (Fig. 5a, dotted lines). Fig. 5b illustrates the distinctive color responses of the three CDs to TA and furfural, respectively, both under sunlight (upper) and UV light (bottom) irradiation. The significant differences observed were attributed to the varying distributions of amine groups on the surface of the CDs.

Upon the addition of furfural, the ortho-diamino groups situated on the surface of o-CDs are capable of reacting with furfural to form benzimidazole group [50]. The formation of them results in an extension of the electron cloud conjugation within the CDs, which in turn leads to an enhancement and blue-shift of the fluorescence. Being differently, due to the random distribution the amino groups on the surface of m-CDs and p-CDs are unable to form benzimidazole group with furfural. Consequently, no significant changes in fluorescence are observed for them upon the addition of furfural.

Subsequently, we compared the FT-IR spectral changes of o-CDs, o-CDs -TA and o-CDs -TA-F (Fig. 5c). The data reveal that the vibrational band of carbonyl group (νC=O) at 1695 cm−1 is no longer present in the o-CDs-TA-F system, indicating a reaction between the aldehyde group and functional groups on the surface of CDs [51]. Additionally, the significant enhancement of vibrational mode corresponding to C–N band (νC-N) in an aromatic ring at 1286 cm−1 and that of C–H bending model (δC-H) at 699 cm−1 suggests the formation of a nitrogen-containing aromatic ring [52]. These spectral changes are likely associated with the oxidation of amines groups at the surface of CDs.

EPR was employed to identify the active species of
· SO
4 ‐
and
· OH
involved in the TA activation systems. In these experiments, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was used as a capture agent (Fig. 5d). The presence of the characteristic peaks corresponding to of DMPO-
· OH
and DMPO-
· SO
4 ‐
specifies the formation of
· OH
and
· SO
4 ‐
[53] in the solution of TA in the presence of o-CDs-TA. The free radical generated from the TA pair abstract protons from the amine group, thereby activating them to form iminium-ion intermediates at the surface of CDs [17]. The FT-IR spectral changes observed for m-CDs and p-CDs in response to TA and furfural (Figure S4) confirm the aforementioned molecular-levels interactions. Consequently, it is the ortho-diamine groups on the surface of o-CDs that reacted with furfural to form the furan-based imidazole. Collectively, these indicated that the distribution of amine sites on the CDs’ surface is critical for their reaction with furfural.

Additionally, the Zeta-potentials measurements (Figure S5) provide further evidence supporting this conclusion. After the addition of furfural, o-CDs-TA-F exhibited a lower potential than o-CDs-TA and furfural, suggesting that a combination between o-CDs and furfural indeed occurred. Along with the formation of o-CDs-TA-F, the color of solution changed from orange to green. Meanwhile, the emission lifetime increased from 1.91 ns (at 570 nm for o-CDs) to 4.44 ns (at 520 nm for final product; Figure S6), indicating the generation of new fluorescent chromophore in product.

Hence, in an environment rich in active free radicals, the amino groups of o-CDs can be effectively activated to form iminium-ion species, which are susceptible to reacting with furfural. This reaction leads to the formation of a furan-based imidazole structure at the CDs’ surface through an elimination oxidation process. Furthermore, the new structure increases the electron density at the surface of the CDs, ultimately inducing a blue-shift in the emission of o-CDs and resulting in the emergence of a new fluorescence peak at 520 nm (Scheme 2 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The determination of furfural in Chinese Baijiu
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to achieve accurate furfural determination in Chinese Baijiu, it is imperative to assess the impact of ethanol on the performance of the developed detection method. As depicted in Fig. 6 a (connected lines), the emission intensity of o-CDs-TA increases with the addition of ethanol to the detection system, attributable to the slight solvent effect of ethanol on CDs as previously reported [54]. This increase is denoted as ΔI
1 (Fig. 6b, blue). Furthermore, the presence of furfural in water causes a significant enhancement in the emission of o-CDs-TA (black, from connected to dashed line). However, unlike o-CDs-TA, the emission intensity of o-CDs-TA-F decreases with the addition of more ethanol (Fig. 6a, dashed lines), and the reduction is termed ΔI
2 (Fig. 6b, black). The bursting effect of ethanol on o-CDs-TA-F may be related to the competitive consumption of OH by ethanol in the system [55]. Taking all these effects into account, it is important to overcome the effect of ethanol when using the newly developed method for the detection of furfural in Baijiu.

Of note, albeit the volume fraction of ethanol in Baijiu is quite high, typically ranged from 40 to 70 vol% depending on alcoholic degree, the high sensitivity of the developed method allows for the use of a small sample volume (<300 μL) of real Baijiu for analysis. Consequently, to facilitate more precise furfural determination in real Baijiu samples by o-CDs-TA, two representative calibration curves were constructed in buffers containing 5 vol % ethanol (Figs. 6c) and 10 vol % ethanol (Fig. 6d), respectively. As depicted, the two curves exhibit excellent linearity within the furfural concentration range of 10–200 μM furfural, and the calculated LOD are 95.0 nM and 146.2 nM, respectively. To detect furfural in Baijiu, the initial step involves diluting the volume fraction of ethanol to 5 % or 10 % should be performed. Furfural concentrations in diluted Baijiu were then measured using an assay based on o-CDs. The furfural concentration in undiluted Baijiu was then recalculated according to the number of times it was diluted. According to available reports, the furfural concentration in Baijiu typically ranges from 50 μM to 20 mM [56]. Therefore, the LOD achieved by o-CDs-TA for furfural are well-suited to meet the requirements for furfural determination in real Baijiu samples, with different ethanol degrees.

The addition experiment was carried out by spiking furfural to the furfural-free Light aroma Baijiu, and the recovery rate was verified for each sample. As shown in Table 1 , the spiking furfural can be well determined using the linear equations for furfural detection containing 5 vol % (Figs. 6c) and 10 vol % ethanol (Fig. 6d). It suggests that neither ethanol nor involved other compounds in Baijiu has effect on the furfural determination using the developed method.

Subsequently, the furfural concentration in five Strong aroma Baijiu was determined for validation purposes (as marked in Fig. 6c and d), respectively. As presented in Table 2 , the determined concentrations of furfural in the five Strong aroma Baijiu are good agreement with the results obtained by HPLC. The accuracy of the method is within the range of 97.4–103.4 % when compared to the results determined by HPLC. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) were then calculated based on the results of five replicate measurements, which are less than 2.7 %. These low RSD values indicate that the developed method can accurately determine the furfural content in Chinese Baijiu samples.

In summary, by employing o-CDs as fluorescence sensor and reactant, furfural is enabled to react with the ortho-diamino groups situated on the surface of o-CDs, with the assistance of APS and TMEDA in generating free radicals. This process alters the original orange luminescent (570 nm) of o-CDs to green (520 nm), thereby, providing a visual means of detecting furfural. The detection of furfural through this specific reaction results in a wide responsive range of 10–200 μM and a low detection limit of 30.5 nM. Finally, the method was successfully applied to the determination of furfural in Chinese Baijiu, yielding satisfactory results and demonstrating its high applicability. The mechanism study suggests that the ortho-diamino groups on the surface of o-CDs are essential for condensing/reacting with the carbonyl in furfural, while the free radicals generated by APS and TMEDA are particularly crucial. Consequently, this study presents a novel and highly selective fluorescence-enhanced method for the determination of furfural, achieved the direct reaction between furfural and the surface functional groups on CDs, leading to the fluorescence signal changes.

Hao-Jie Bai: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Hong-Wei Li: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Yi Li: Funding acquisition. Zhijiu Huang: Resources. Sha Liu: Resources. Xin-He Duan: Methodology, Investigation. Yuqing Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial supports from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21875085) and Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Development Plan Project (No. 20230204040YY) were appreciated.
